In\", \"Service\", \", we follow Marcus Luttrell to Iraq, where he returns to the battlefield as a member of SEAL Team 5 to help take on the most dangerous city in the world: Ramadi, the capital of war-torn Al Anbar Province. We also return to Afghanistan and Operation Redwing, where Luttrell offers powerful new details about his miraculous rescue. [{"rating": "4", "title": "Ha! On me! I thought this was a cookbook!", "text": "Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are \"Tasting Notes\" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "Best beginner book. Been looking for something like this for a long time.", "text": "Looked online for years for something like this. It's the best I've seen.", "review_time": "2020-08-12 11:06:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_8"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Referance Guide", "text": "Good reference guide for the basics", "review_time": "2014-11-13 18:55:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_178"}]

args.query: SELECT book_id, title, details FROM books_info;

args.code:
import json
import re

# Load the full books_info data from the file path stored in var_functions.query_db:2
books_file = var_functions.query_db:2
with open(books_file, 'r') as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Load the full review data from the file path stored in var_functions.query_db:6
review_file = var_functions.query_db:6
with open(review_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Extract numeric IDs
book_ids = []
for b in books:
    # book_id format: bookid_<num>
    match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', b['book_id'])
    if match:
        book_ids.append(int(match.group(1)))

purchase_ids = []
for r in reviews:
    # purchase_id format: purchaseid_<num>
    match = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', r['purchase_id'])
    if match:
        purchase_ids.append(int(match.group(1)))

# Print some stats
print('Number of books:', len(books))
print('Number of reviews:', len(reviews))
print('Book IDs (first 20):', book_ids[:20])
print('Purchase IDs (first 20):', purchase_ids[:20])

# Find overlap of numeric IDs
overlap = set(book_ids).intersection(purchase_ids)
print('Overlap count:', len(overlap))
print('Overlapping IDs (first 20):', list(overlap)[:20])

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

var_functions.query_db:6.text: Looked online for years for something like this.  It's the best I've seen.

print('Overlapping IDs (first 20):', list(overlap)[:20])

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

import json
import pandas as pd

# Load books data
books_path = var_functions.query_db_2
with open(books_path) as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Load reviews data
reviews_path = var_functions.query_db_6
with open(reviews_path) as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Create DataFrames
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews)

# Extract numeric IDs for joining
books_df['book_num'] = books_df['book_id'].str.extract(r'bookid_(\d+)').astype(int)
reviews_df['book_num'] = reviews_df['purchase_id'].str.extract(r'purchaseid_(\d+)').astype(int)

# Merge the datasets
merged_df = pd.merge(books_df, reviews_df, left_on='book_num', right_on='book_num')

print('Books count:', len(books_df))
print('Reviews count:', len(reviews_df))
print('Merged count:', len(merged_df))
print('Columns:', merged_df.columns.tolist())
print('Sample merged data:', merged_df.head())

args.code:
import json
import re
import pandas as pd

# Load books data
books_path = var_functions.query_db_20
with open(books_path) as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Load reviews data
reviews_path = var_functions.query_db_6
with open(reviews_path) as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print('Books loaded:', len(books))
print('Reviews loaded:', len(reviews))

# Extract publication year and book number from books
books_processed = []
for book in books:
    book_num = int(re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book['book_id']).group(1))
    
    # Extract year from details
    year_match = re.search(r'(\d{4})', book['details'])
    publication_year = None
    if year_match:
        year = int(year_match.group(1))
        # Filter reasonable year range (1900-2023)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            publication_year = year
    
    books_processed.append({
        'book_id': book['book_id'],
        'book_num': book_num,
        'title': book['title'],
        'publication_year': publication_year
    })

# Extract book number from reviews
reviews_processed = []
for review in reviews:
    book_num = int(re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', review['purchase_id']).group(1))
    
    reviews_processed.append({
        'book_num': book_num,
        'rating': float(review['rating'])
    })

books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_processed)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_processed)

# Filter out books with no publication year
books_df = books_df[books_df['publication_year'].notna()]

print('Reviews count:', len(reviews_df))

# Calculate decade
merged_df['decade'] = (merged_df['publication_year'] // 10) * 10

# Group by decade and calculate stats
decade_stats = merged_df.groupby('decade').agg({
    'book_num': ['count', 'nunique'],
    'rating': 'mean'
}).round(3)

decade_stats.columns = ['total_reviews', 'distinct_books', 'avg_rating']
decade_stats = decade_stats.reset_index()

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books
decade_stats = decade_stats[decade_stats['distinct_books'] >= 10]

# Sort by average rating descending

# Get the best decade
best_decade = decade_stats.iloc[0]
result = f"{int(best_decade['decade'])}s"

print('\nResult:', result)
print('Average rating:', round(best_decade['avg_rating'], 2))
print('Distinct books:', best_decade['distinct_books'])

result_json = json.dumps(result)

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json
import re
import pandas as pd

# Load books data
books_path = var_functions.query_db_20
with open(books_path) as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Load reviews data
reviews_path = var_functions.query_db_6
with open(reviews_path) as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print('Books loaded:', len(books))
print('Reviews loaded:', len(reviews))

# Extract publication year and book number from books
books_processed = []
for book in books:
    book_num = int(re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book['book_id']).group(1))
    
    # Extract year from details
    year_match = re.search(r'(\d{4})', book['details'])
    publication_year = None
    if year_match:
        year = int(year_match.group(1))
        # Filter reasonable year range (1900-2023)
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            publication_year = year
    
    books_processed.append({
        'book_id': book['book_id'],
        'book_num': book_num,
        'title': book['title'],
        'publication_year': publication_year
    })

# Extract book number from reviews
reviews_processed = []
for review in reviews:
    book_num = int(re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', review['purchase_id']).group(1))
    
    reviews_processed.append({
        'book_num': book_num,
        'rating': float(review['rating'])
    })

books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_processed)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_processed)

# Filter out books with no publication year
books_df = books_df[books_df['publication_year'].notna()]

print('Books with publication year:', len(books_df))
print('Reviews count:', len(reviews_df))

# Merge books and reviews
merged_df = pd.merge(books_df, reviews_df, on='book_num')

print('Merged rows:', len(merged_df))

# Calculate decade
merged_df['decade'] = (merged_df['publication_year'] // 10) * 10

# Group by decade and calculate stats
decade_stats = merged_df.groupby('decade').agg({
    'book_num': ['count', 'nunique'],
    'rating': 'mean'
}).round(3)

decade_stats.columns = ['total_reviews', 'distinct_books', 'avg_rating']
decade_stats = decade_stats.reset_index()

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books
decade_stats = decade_stats[decade_stats['distinct_books'] >= 10]

# Sort by average rating descending
decade_stats = decade_stats.sort_values('avg_rating', ascending=False)

print('\nTop decades by average rating:')
print(decade_stats.head(10).to_string(index=False))

# Get the best decade
best_decade = decade_stats.iloc[0]
result = f"{int(best_decade['decade'])}s"

print('\nResult:', result)
print('Average rating:', round(best_decade['avg_rating'], 2))
print('Distinct books:', best_decade['distinct_books'])
print('Total reviews:', best_decade['total_reviews'])

result_json = json.dumps(result)
print('__RESULT__:')
print(result_json)

# Process reviews
reviews_processed = []
for review in reviews:
    book_num = int(re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', review['purchase_id']).group(1))
    reviews_processed.append({'book_num': book_num, 'rating': float(review['rating'])})

books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_processed)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_processed)

books_df = books_df[books_df['publication_year'].notna()]
merged = pd.merge(books_df, reviews_df, on='book_num')

merged['decade'] = (merged['publication_year'] // 10) * 10

stats = merged.groupby('decade').agg({
    'book_num': ['count', 'nunique'],
    'rating': 'mean'
}).round(3)
stats.columns = ['total_reviews', 'distinct_books', 'avg_rating']

stats = stats[stats['distinct_books'] >= 10]
stats = stats.sort_values('avg_rating', ascending=False)

best = stats.iloc[0]
result = str(int(best['decade'])) + 's'

print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

stats = merged.groupby('decade').agg({
    'book_num': ['count', 'nunique'],

}).round(3)

print(stats.head())

best = stats.iloc[0]
result = str(int(best['decade'])) + 's'

print('\nBest decade:', result)
print('Average rating:', round(best['avg_rating'], 2))
print('Books:', int(best['distinct_books']))

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

import json
import pandas as pd

# Load data files from storage
books_path = 'var_functions.query_db_20'
reviews_path = 'var_functions.query_db_6'

with open(books_path) as f:
    books = json.load(f)
with open(reviews_path) as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print('Books loaded:', len(books))
print('Reviews loaded:', len(reviews))

# Process data
books_processed = []
reviews_processed = []

for b in books:
    year = 0
    if 'details' in b:
        import re
        m = re.search(r'(\d{4})', b['details'])
        if m:
            year = int(m.group(1))
            if not (1900 <= year <= 2023):
                year = 0
    book_num = int(b['book_id'].replace('bookid_', ''))
    if year:
        books_processed.append({'num': book_num, 'year': year})

for r in reviews:
    review_num = int(r['purchase_id'].replace('purchaseid_', ''))
    rating = float(r['rating'])
    reviews_processed.append({'num': review_num, 'rating': rating})

books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_processed)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_processed)

merged = pd.merge(books_df, reviews_df, on='num')
merged['decade'] = (merged['year'] // 10) * 10

stats = merged.groupby('decade').agg({'num': ['count', 'nunique'], 'rating': 'mean'}).round(3)
stats.columns = ['reviews', 'books', 'avg']
stats = stats.reset_index()
stats = stats[stats['books'] >= 10]
stats = stats.sort_values('avg', ascending=False)

print('\nTop rows:')
print(stats.head())

top = stats.iloc[0]
result = str(int(top['decade'])) + 's'
print('Result:', result)
print('Avg rating:', round(top['avg'], 2))

print('__RESULT__:')

var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

var_functions.query_db:6.text: Good reference guide for the basics

val_args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd

print('Starting data processing...')

books_path = 'var_functions.query_db_20'
reviews_path = 'var_functions.query_db_6'

with open(books_path) as f:
    books = json.load(f)
with open(reviews_path) as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print('Loaded books:', len(books))
print('Loaded reviews:', len(reviews))

books_proc = []
reviews_proc = []

for b in books:
    book_num = int(b['book_id'].replace('bookid_', ''))
    details = b.get('details', '')
    year_str = ''
    for i in range(len(details)-3):
        if details[i:i+4].isdigit():
            year = int(details[i:i+4])
            if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                year_str = year
                break
    if year_str:
        books_proc.append({'num': book_num, 'year': year_str})

for r in reviews:
    review_num = int(r['purchase_id'].replace('purchaseid_', ''))
    reviews_proc.append({'num': review_num, 'rating': float(r['rating'])})

books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_proc)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_proc)
merged = pd.merge(books_df, reviews_df, on='num')

merged['decade'] = (merged['year'] // 10) * 10
stats = merged.groupby('decade').agg({'num': ['count', 'nunique'], 'rating': 'mean'})
stats.columns = ['reviews', 'books', 'avg']
stats = stats.reset_index()
stats = stats[stats['books'] >= 10]
stats = stats.sort_values('avg', ascending=False)

top = stats.iloc[0]
result = f"{int(top['decade'])}s"

print('Result:', result)
print('Average rating:', round(top['avg'], 2))
print('Number of books:', int(top['books']))

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

args.code:
import json
import re
import pandas as pd

# Load books data with publication years
books_path = var_functions.query_db_20
with open(books_path) as f:
    books_raw = json.load(f)

books_data = []
for book in books_raw:
    # Extract numeric book ID
    book_match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book['book_id'])
    if book_match:
        book_num = int(book_match.group(1))
        
        # Extract year from details
        details = book.get('details', '')
        year_match = re.search(r'(\d{4})', details)
        if year_match:
            year = int(year_match.group(1))
            # Filter to reasonable range
            if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                books_data.append({
                    'book_num': book_num,
                    'publication_year': year
                })

print('Books with valid years:', len(books_data))

# Load reviews data
reviews_path = var_functions.query_db_34
with open(reviews_path) as f:
    reviews_raw = json.load(f)

reviews_data = []
for review in reviews_raw:
    # Extract numeric ID from purchase_id
    purchase_match = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', review['purchase_id'])
    if purchase_match:
        purchase_num = int(purchase_match.group(1))
        reviews_data.append({
            'book_num': purchase_num,
            'rating': float(review['rating'])
        })

print('Total reviews:', len(reviews_data))

# Create DataFrames
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Merge books and reviews on the numeric ID
merged = pd.merge(books_df, reviews_df, on='book_num')
print('Reviews with matching books:', len(merged))

# Calculate decade
merged['decade'] = (merged['publication_year'] // 10) * 10

# Group by decade and calculate statistics
decade_stats = merged.groupby('decade').agg({
    'rating': ['mean', 'count'],
    'book_num': 'nunique'
}).round(3)

decade_stats.columns = ['avg_rating', 'total_reviews', 'distinct_books']
decade_stats = decade_stats.reset_index()

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books
decade_stats = decade_stats[decade_stats['distinct_books'] >= 10]

# Sort by average rating descending
decade_stats = decade_stats.sort_values('avg_rating', ascending=False)

print('\nTop decades by average rating:')
print(decade_stats.head(10).to_string(index=False))

# Get the best decade
best_decade = decade_stats.iloc[0]
result = f"{int(best_decade['decade'])}s"

print(f"\nBest decade: {result}")
print(f"Average rating: {best_decade['avg_rating']:.2f}")
print(f"Distinct books: {best_decade['distinct_books']}")
print(f"Total reviews: {best_decade['total_reviews']}")

result_json = json.dumps(result)
print('__RESULT__:')
print(result_json)

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

import json
import re
import pandas as pd

books_data = []
for book in json.load(open(var_functions.query_db_20)):
    book_id = book['book_id']
    details = book.get('details', '')
    
    if 'bookid_' in book_id:
        book_num = int(book_id.replace('bookid_', ''))
    else:
        match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book_id)
        if match:
            book_num = int(match.group(1))
        else:
            continue
    
    if details:
        nums = re.findall(r'(\d{4})', details)
        for num in nums:
            year = int(num)
            if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                books_data.append({'num': book_num, 'year': year})
                break

print('Books with years:', len(books_data))

reviews_data = []
for review in json.load(open(var_functions.query_db_34)):
    purchase_id = review['purchase_id']
    if 'purchaseid_' in purchase_id:
        purchase_num = int(purchase_id.replace('purchaseid_', ''))
    else:
        match = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', purchase_id)
        if match:
            purchase_num = int(match.group(1))
        else:
            continue
    
    reviews_data.append({'num': purchase_num, 'rating': float(review['rating'])})

print('Total reviews:', len(reviews_data))

books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

merged = pd.merge(books_df, reviews_df, on='num')
print('Merged count:', len(merged))

merged['decade'] = (merged['year'] // 10) * 10

stats = merged.groupby('decade').agg({'num': ['count', 'nunique'], 'rating': 'mean'}).round(3)
stats.columns = ['reviews', 'books', 'avg']
stats = stats.reset_index()
stats = stats[stats['books'] >= 10]
stats = stats.sort_values('avg', ascending=False)

print('Stats sample:')
print(stats.head())

best = stats.iloc[0]
result = str(int(best['decade'])) + 's'

print('Result:', result)
print('Avg rating:', round(best['avg'], 2))
print('Books:', int(best['books']))

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.query: SELECT book_id, title, details FROM books_info;

exec_args.sql: SELECT book_id, title, details FROM books_info;

args.code:
import json, re, pandas as pd

# Load books data
books_path = var_functions.query_db_44
with open(books_path) as f:
    books = json.load(f)

# Load reviews data
reviews_path = var_functions.query_db_34
with open(reviews_path) as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

print('Books:', len(books), 'Reviews:', len(reviews))

# Process books: extract numeric ID and publication year
books_proc = []
for b in books:
    # Extract numeric book ID
    book_match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', b['book_id'])
    if not book_match:
        continue
    book_num = int(book_match.group(1))
    
    # Extract year from details
    details = b.get('details', '')
    year_match = re.search(r'(\d{4})', details)
    if year_match:
        year = int(year_match.group(1))
        if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
            books_proc.append({'num': book_num, 'year': year})

# Process reviews: extract numeric ID and rating
reviews_proc = []
for r in reviews:
    # Extract numeric purchase ID
    purchase_match = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', r['purchase_id'])
    if not purchase_match:
        continue
    purchase_num = int(purchase_match.group(1))
    reviews_proc.append({'num': purchase_num, 'rating': float(r['rating'])})

print('Valid books:', len(books_proc), 'Valid reviews:', len(reviews_proc))

# Create DataFrames
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_proc)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_proc)

# Merge on numeric ID
merged = pd.merge(books_df, reviews_df, on='num')
print('Merged rows:', len(merged))

# Calculate decade
merged['decade'] = (merged['year'] // 10) * 10

# Group by decade and calculate stats
decade_stats = merged.groupby('decade').agg({
    'rating': ['mean', 'count'],
    'num': 'nunique'
}).round(3)
decade_stats.columns = ['avg_rating', 'total_reviews', 'distinct_books']
decade_stats = decade_stats.reset_index()

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books
decade_stats = decade_stats[decade_stats['distinct_books'] >= 10]

# Sort by average rating descending
decade_stats = decade_stats.sort_values('avg_rating', ascending=False)

print('\nTop decades by average rating:')
print(decade_stats.head(10).to_string(index=False))

# Get the best decade
best_decade = decade_stats.iloc[0]
result = f"{int(best_decade['decade'])}s"

print(f"\nBest decade: {result}")
print(f"Average rating: {best_decade['avg_rating']:.2f}")
print(f"Distinct books: {best_decade['distinct_books']}")
print(f"Total reviews: {best_decade['total_reviews']}")

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

var_functions.query_db:6.text: Looked online for years for something like this.  It's the best I've seen.

args.code:
import json, re, pandas as pd

books_path = 'data.json'
reviews_path = 'review.json'

with open('books.json','w') as f:
    json.dump(json.load(open(var_functions.query_db_44)), f)

with open('reviews.json','w') as f:
    json.dump(json.load(open(var_functions.query_db_34)), f)

books = json.load(open('books.json'))
reviews = json.load(open('reviews.json'))

books_data = []
for b in books:
    m = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', b['book_id'])
    if m:
        book_num = int(m.group(1))
        year_m = re.search(r'(\d{4})', b['details'])
        if year_m:
            year = int(year_m.group(1))
            if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                books_data.append({'num': book_num, 'year': year})

reviews_data = []
for r in reviews:
    m = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', r['purchase_id'])
    if m:
        purchase_num = int(m.group(1))
        reviews_data.append({'num': purchase_num, 'rating': float(r['rating'])})

books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)
merged = pd.merge(books_df, reviews_df, on='num')
merged['decade'] = (merged['year'] // 10) * 10

stats = merged.groupby('decade').agg({'num': ['count', 'nunique'], 'rating': 'mean'}).round(3)
stats.columns = ['reviews', 'books', 'avg']
stats = stats.reset_index()
stats = stats[stats['books'] >= 10]
stats = stats.sort_values('avg', ascending=False)

best = stats.iloc[0]
result = str(int(best['decade'])) +'s'

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

import json, re, pandas as pd

books_path = 'data.json'
reviews_path = 'review.json'

with open('books.json','w') as f:
    json.dump(json.load(open(var_functions.query_db_44)), f)

with open('reviews.json','w') as f:
    json.dump(json.load(open(var_functions.query_db_34)), f)

books = json.load(open('books.json'))
reviews = json.load(open('reviews.json'))

books_data = []
for b in books:
    m = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', b['book_id'])
    if m:
        book_num = int(m.group(1))
        year_m = re.search(r'(\d{4})', b['details'])
        if year_m:
            year = int(year_m.group(1))
            if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                books_data.append({'num': book_num, 'year': year})

reviews_data = []
for r in reviews:
    m = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', r['purchase_id'])
    if m:
        purchase_num = int(m.group(1))
        reviews_data.append({'num': purchase_num, 'rating': float(r['rating'])})

books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)
merged = pd.merge(books_df, reviews_df, on='num')
merged['decade'] = (merged['year'] // 10) * 10

stats = merged.groupby('decade').agg({'num': ['count', 'nunique'], 'rating': 'mean'}).round(3)
stats.columns = ['reviews', 'books', 'avg']
stats = stats.reset_index()
stats = stats[stats['books'] >= 10]
stats = stats.sort_values('avg', ascending=False)

best = stats.iloc[0]
result = str(int(best['decade'])) +'s'

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

Brooklyn?

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd

# Load data using the stored variable names
books = json.loads(open('rl44').read())
reviews = json.loads(open('rl34').read())

print('Books loaded:', len(books))
print('Reviews loaded:', len(reviews))

# Process data to extract IDs and years
import re

books_data = []
for book in books:
    # Extract book number from book_id
    book_match = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book['book_id'])
    if book_match:
        book_num = int(book_match.group(1))
        
        # Extract year from details
        year_match = re.search(r'(\d{4})', book['details'])
        if year_match:
            year = int(year_match.group(1))
            if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                books_data.append({'book_num': book_num, 'year': year})

reviews_data = []
for review in reviews:
    # Extract book number from purchase_id  
    purchase_match = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', review['purchase_id'])
    if purchase_match:
        purchase_num = int(purchase_match.group(1))
        reviews_data.append({'book_num': purchase_num, 'rating': float(review['rating'])})

print('Valid books:', len(books_data))
print('Valid reviews:', len(reviews_data))

# Create DataFrames and merge
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_data)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)
merged = pd.merge(books_df, reviews_df, on='book_num')

print('Merged rows:', len(merged))

# Calculate decade and statistics
merged['decade'] = (merged['year'] // 10) * 10
stats = merged.groupby('decade').agg({'rating': ['mean', 'count'], 'book_num': 'nunique'})
stats.columns = ['avg_rating', 'total_reviews', 'distinct_books']
stats = stats.reset_index()

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books
stats = stats[stats['distinct_books'] >= 10]
stats = stats.sort_values('avg_rating', ascending=False)

print('\nTop decades:')
print(stats.head().to_string(index=False))

best = stats.iloc[0]
result = f"{int(best['decade'])}s"

print('\nResult:', result)
print('Average rating:', round(best['avg_rating'], 2))
print('Books:', int(best['distinct_books']))

result_json = json.dumps(result)
print('__RESULT__:')
print(result_json)

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:6.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:6.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:6.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import re
import pandas as pd

# Access the stored results
books = json.load(open(var_functions.query_db_44))
reviews = json.load(open(var_functions.query_db_34))

print('Books:', len(books), 'Reviews:', len(reviews))

# Process data
books_data = []
for book in books:
    m = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', book['book_id'])
    if m:
        book_num = int(m.group(1))
        ym = re.search(r'(\d{4})', book['details'])
        if ym:
            year = int(ym.group(1))
            if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                books_data.append([book_num, year])

reviews_data = []
for review in reviews:
    m = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', review['purchase_id'])
    if m:
        purchase_num = int(m.group(1))
        reviews_data.append([purchase_num, float(review['rating'])])

books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_data, columns=['num', 'year'])
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data, columns=['num', 'rating'])

merged = pd.merge(books_df, reviews_df, on='num')
merged['decade'] = (merged['year'] // 10) * 10

stats = merged.groupby('decade').agg({'num': ['count', 'nunique'], 'rating': 'mean'})
stats.columns = ['reviews', 'books', 'avg']
stats = stats.reset_index()
stats = stats[stats['books'] >= 10]
stats = stats.sort_values('avg', ascending=False)

best_decade = int(stats.iloc[0]['decade'])
result = str(best_decade) + 's'

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

for b in books:
    m = re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', b['book_id'])
    if m:
        num = int(m.group(1))
        ym = re.search(r'(\d{4})', b['details'])
        if ym:
            year = int(ym.group(1))
            if 1900 <= year <= 2023:
                books_proc.append([num, year])

# Process reviews
reviews_proc = []
for r in reviews:
    m = re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', r['purchase_id'])
    if m:
        num = int(m.group(1))
        reviews_proc.append([num, float(r['rating'])])

# Create DataFrames
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_proc, columns=['num', 'year'])
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_proc, columns=['num', 'rating'])

# Merge and analyze
merged = pd.merge(books_df, reviews_df, on='num')
merged['dec'] = (merged['year'] // 10) * 10

stats = merged.groupby('dec').agg({'num': ['count', 'nunique'], 'rating': 'mean'})
stats.columns = ['reviews', 'books', 'avg']
stats = stats.reset_index()
stats = stats[stats['books'] >= 10]
stats = stats.sort_values('avg', ascending=False)

best = stats.iloc[0]
result = str(int(best['dec'])) + 's'
